colaAssumptions.summary: US ≤3%; CA ≤3%; PH ≤3%; UK ≤3%; NL ≤3%; DE ≤3% — Capped

flags: Pricing workbook applies rates to current headcount without efficiency ramp — trajectory rises YoY; not a cost-reduction posture vs baseline.
$3M exclusivity payment to FIS and $2M tech migration called out in materials — still well above FIS baseline on TCV.
evaluationBriefText:
UBIQUITY
Vendor Evaluation Brief
FIS TMS Managed Services RFP | March 30, 2026 | Confidential
1. Vendor Files Identified
Ubiquity_FIS__Ubiquity_Proposal.pdf (36 pages)
Ubiquity_Pricing_Response_for_FIS2.xlsx (1 tab: 6.0 Pricing)
Ubiquity_Response_Vendor_RFP_Questionnaire_with_Fraud_Supplement_FINAL.xlsx (108-question questionnaire)
Ubiquity_Appendix_C_-_Draft_FIS_SOW_RF_redliines_CB.docx (SOW redlines)
Ubiquity_Executive_Summary_UbiquityFIS.pdf (Exec summary)
NOTE: Standard Appendix B NOT submitted. Tabs 5.0, 7.0, 9.0 missing.
2. Evaluation Tear Sheet
Total data points: 32
3. Completeness Summary
Total data points: 32
4. Executive Assessment Memo
UBIQUITY
Bottom Line
Ubiquity’s proposal is fundamentally non-competitive on a cost basis: the 5-year TCV of $938.7M is nearly double the next most expensive vendor (Sutherland at $559.7M) and costs $188.1M MORE than FIS’s current baseline over 5 years. Costs increase year-over-year from $176.8M to $199.0M due to compounding 3% COLA on an already-high rate card. Ubiquity did not submit a standard Appendix B (missing Tabs 5.0, 7.0, and 9.0), making structured evaluation against the 15-subcomponent framework impossible for large portions of the assessment. The proposal positions Ubiquity as a "managed services transformation partner" focused on stabilization and SLA improvement rather than cost reduction — a fundamentally different value proposition than what the RFP requires. Ubiquity may have niche value for specific queues or as a secondary provider but cannot serve as the primary outsourcing partner at these economics.
Underlying Assumptions in their Proposal
Pricing model: Rate-card only (fixed fee rows all "NA"). Rates are quoted as text strings ("US: $40.95") rather than numeric values, making automated comparison difficult. The financial summary uses Appendix D FTE counts multiplied by hourly rates — essentially a headcount-times-rate model with no structural cost reduction. (Tab 6.0 Pricing C54–V56; C13–I34)
No offshoring, minimal AI: Ubiquity proposes retaining current servicing locations with optional future adjustment. There is no structured efficiency tab (9.0 not submitted), no offshore:onshore target, and no labor arbitrage mechanism. AI is described as "15–30% efficiency improvement over 3 years" in the proposal but has no structured data backing. (Proposal p.4–5)
Different value proposition: Ubiquity explicitly positions itself as "not a traditional outsourcing model" and "not a system replacement initiative." The proposal focuses on SLA stabilization, service quality improvement, and operational continuity — not the $40M exit-run-rate cost synergy that FIS is targeting. (Proposal p.5)
Minimum volume: 100% of existing business for both NA and EMEA, plus 100% rebadging — the most restrictive conditions in the field. (Tab 6.0 Pricing C81–D83)
Strengths & Differentiators
Best COLA structure in the field: "Lesser of in-country inflation or 3%" provides the strongest inflation protection of any vendor. This cap limits long-term cost escalation risk. (Tab 6.0 Pricing C41–C46)
$3M exclusivity payment to FIS: The only vendor proposing an upfront cash payment to FIS (vs. vendor-funded investments that benefit the vendor’s own operations). (Tab 6.0 Pricing C73)
Zero one-time costs to FIS: No setup, rebadging, training, dual-run, or licensing costs. This is the cleanest cost structure — what FIS pays is purely the rate card. (Tab 6.0 Pricing C66–C74)
Multi-geography delivery presence: Locations in US, El Salvador, Philippines, India, Ghana, UK, Colombia, Honduras provide flexibility, including emerging market locations (Ghana) not offered by other vendors. (Proposal p.6)
Key Risks & Concerns
$938.7M TCV is nearly 2x any other vendor and $188.1M MORE than doing nothing: This is not a savings proposal. FIS would spend an additional $188.1M over 5 years versus its current baseline. The ~5% "savings" Ubiquity claims are measured against their own inflated cost model, not FIS’s actual spend. (Tab 6.0 Pricing C112–S112)
Missing structured data across multiple evaluation dimensions: No Tab 5.0 (Technology), No Tab 7.0 (Client Migration), No Tab 9.0 (Efficiency Assumptions). The questionnaire and proposal PDF partially address these areas qualitatively, but structured comparison against the 15-subcomponent framework is impossible. (Master prompt; file inventory)
No pathway to the $40M exit-run-rate synergy target: The proposal contains no offshoring plan, no labor arbitrage mechanism, and no AI/digital quantification that would deliver the cost reduction FIS requires. Even with the best COLA cap in the field, costs increase every year. (Calculated; Proposal p.3–5)
Mid-market scale may be insufficient: At 200+ clients and a delivery footprint significantly smaller than Cognizant (351K), Genpact (140K), or IBM, Ubiquity may lack the organizational capacity to absorb 2,678 FTE across 6 countries simultaneously. (Proposal p.6)
Key Questions to Pressure-Test in Workshop
Your 5-year TCV is $938.7M versus a next-closest bid of $460M. What structural changes to your model would reduce TCV to within the competitive range of $450–560M?
You position this as "not a traditional outsourcing model." If FIS’s primary objective is $40M annual cost synergy by FY28, is Ubiquity the right partner? What alternative value proposition would you make?
Your efficiency target is 15–30% over 3 years. Provide the specific AI tools, FTE impact, and dollar savings by year that underpin this range.
You require 100% of existing business. Are you willing to participate in a partial scope award (e.g., EMEA only, or specific service towers)?
Go-Forward Implications
Ubiquity’s proposal validates the cost floor: at current locations and rates with no offshoring or AI, the TMS operation costs ~$177M/year. This confirms that savings MUST come from labor arbitrage and AI/digital — operational improvements alone cannot deliver the FIS target.
Ubiquity’s COLA cap structure ("lesser of inflation or 3%") is the best in the field and should be adopted as the standard for all vendor contracts regardless of who is selected.
If FIS has specific queues or geographies where SLA stabilization (not cost reduction) is the priority, Ubiquity could serve as a supplemental provider — but not as the primary outsourcing partner at these economics.

[Questionnaire excerpts]
Ubiquity is not a subsidiary and operates as an independent, privately held company.
Ubiquity will provide copies of its most recent audited financial statements for each of the last three completed fiscal years through a secure email address provided by the customer, and will share the information and documents using secured email for compliance purposes.
Ubiquity supports phone as a primary channel and can also support omnichannel service, including in-app, email, and chat.
It integrates with client programs as an extension of the client’s team, using existing tools while addressing security issues prior to launch and aligning on required tools, websites, and workflows .
If a client does not have a telephony system, Ubiquity provides an AVAYA-based infrastructure that supports inbound and outbound communications, hosts its own IVRs, and can create complex call flows for caller authentication and self-service .
It also supports integration with major softphone platforms, including Twilio Flex, Zendesk, Genesys, TalkDesk, and Amazon Connect, and can accommodate client-hosted or managed telephony platforms .
The inTouch® system provides agents with visibility into the caller’s IVR path and supports real-time monitoring, reporting, and analytics, including call recording, agent availability tracking, and secure access controls.
568 Broadway, #601 New York, NY 10012.
Ubiquity is headquartered in New York, NY.
2) If a question is not applicable, enter 'N/A' and briefly explain why.
3) When attachments are requested, provide a URL/link or file name in the Attachment(s)/URL(s) column.
4) For 'Yes/No + Narrative' items, select Yes/No/N/A in the Y/N/N/A column and add supporting detail in the Narrative column.
5) If you need more space, add rows below the question and repeat the Question ID.
Answer each question. Yellow cells are for vendor input.
Provide a brief overview of your company, including ownership structure, years in operation, and primary service offerings. Ubiquity is a privately held business process outsourcing (BPO) provider founded in 2012, with 14 years of operational experience. The company specializes in customer experience (CX), back-office operations, and risk & compliance services, supporting global clients across multiple industries.

Primary service offerings include:

• Customer Experience (CX): Omnichannel support (voice, chat, email, SMS), technical support, sales, retention, and collections.
• Risk & Compliance: Identity verification, fraud prevention, chargeback management, and regulatory support.
• Back-Office Services: Data processing, reporting, analytics, and operational support.
• Consulting & Optimization: Workforce management, QA design, process improvement, and digital transformation.
• Global Delivery: 24/7/365 operations across onshore, nearshore, and offshore sites with multilingual capabilities.

Ubiquity delivers flexible engagement models and scalable operations supported by technology, analytics, and experienced leadership teams.
Provide your financial stability overview (e.g., most recent annual revenue range, profitability trend) and confirm ability to support multi-year engagement. Ubiquity is financially stable and supports long-term, multi-year client engagements across global operations.

While detailed financial statements are shared under NDA during contracting, Ubiquity maintains consistent revenue growth, strong client retention, and profitability across its portfolio.

The company has demonstrated the ability to scale large programs (e.g., ramping 300 to 1,000 FTEs) and invest in infrastructure, technology, and talent to support enterprise clients over multi-year engagements.
List your primary contact center delivery locations (country/city), including seat capacity and language capabilities. Ubiquity operates a global delivery network across the Americas, EMEA, and APAC regions:

United States
• New York, NY  
• Mission, TX

LATAM
• Bogotá, Colombia  
• San Salvador, El Salvador (2 sites)  
• San Pedro Sula, Honduras  
• Mérida, Mexico  
• Guatemala City, Guatemala  

EMEA
• Accra, Ghana  
• Manchester, United Kingdom  

APAC
• Manila, Philippines (2 sites)  
• Laguna, Philippines  
• Bacolod, Philippines (2 sites)  
• Cagayan de Oro, Philippines  

Langauge Capabilities
English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Tagalog, Japanese, Korean, Mandarin, Cantonese, Hindi, Arabic, and other regional languages.

Capacity scales dynamically based on program needs across sites.
Describe your experience supporting U.S.-based consumer financial services (e.g., lending, servicing, collections). Include years of experience and client examples. Ubiquity has over a decade of experience (since 2012) supporting U.S.-based consumer financial services clients, including fintechs, digital lenders, and credit providers.

Lending & Servicing
• Supports $50B+ in managed loan portfolios  
• Processes 2M+ monthly interactions  
• Services include onboarding, verification, servicing, and portfolio transfers  

Collections
• Supporting collections since 2015  
• Achieved up to 15% improvement in collection rates  
• Compliance-first approach aligned with FDCPA  

Card & Account Services
• End-to-end credit card servicing (activation, disputes, payments, rewards)  

Ubiquity combines operational scale with regulatory compliance and customer-centric delivery.
Provide at least three relevant client references for comparable scope (voice + back office and/or staff augmentation).
Describe your proposed operating model for this engagement (staff augmentation vs managed services vs hybrid). Ubiquity supports flexible operating models, including staff augmentation, managed services, and hybrid delivery.

For this engagement, Ubiquity proposes a staffing model comprised of local and gloabl support combining:
• Dedicated teams aligned to client processes and KPIs  
• Embedded leadership and QA oversight  
• Scalable staffing across global delivery sites  

This approach enables operational control, flexibility, and cost efficiency while maintaining consistent service quality.

Delivery can be fully onshore, offshore, or blended based on client requirements.
Confirm ability to support the hours of operation (Mon–Fri 7:00am–11:00pm CT; Sat 7:00am–5:00pm CT) and describe any constraints. Ubiquity can fully support the required hours of operation:

• Monday–Friday: 7:00 AM – 11:00 PM CT  
• Saturday: 7:00 AM – 5:00 PM CT  

All delivery centers operate 24/7/365, allowing flexible scheduling and full leadership coverage aligned to program hours.

No operational constraints are anticipated.
Describe your approach to scaling up/down (seasonality, campaigns, volume spikes) and minimum notice periods required. Ubiquity manages scaling through forecasting, capacity planning, and flexible staffing models.

Scaling Up
• Workforce planning aligned to 12–24 week forecasts  
• Proven ramp: 0–100 FTEs in 90 days; 300–1,000 in 150 days  
• Multi-site recruiting and cross-trained talent pools  

Scaling Down
• Reallocation of staff across programs to minimize attrition  
• Compliance with local labor laws  

Real Time Flexing
• Intraday management with 15–30 minute interval adjustments  
• Cross-skilled agents deployed based on demand  

Standard Notice Period
• Standard: 60–90 days for large-scale ramp planning
Describe how you would structure teams across workgroups (Collections, Customer Service, Chat, Back Office) and how cross-training would be managed. Ubiquity uses a layered team structure to support delivery across workstreams (Collections, Customer Service, Chat, Back Office). We have provided our standards below, but we will build a team and support structure that addresses Santander's specific business needs:

Standard Leadership and Support Ratios
• Team Lead: 1:15  
• QA: 1:50  
• Trainer: 1:75  
• Operations Manager: 1:100  

Additional roles include Workforce Management, SMEs, HR, IT, and Client Success.

Cross-Training Approach
• Struc
